danger_signals.visual_form: Colored blocks advancing toward top of playfield
danger_signals.meaning: Imminent game failure if blocks reach top boundary

danger_signals.visual_form: Blocks rise faster as stage progresses
Gray blocks with bumped/cratered surface texture
danger_signals.meaning: Damage from opponent in competitive modes; blocks opponent must clear

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple blocks fall and cascade after initial match
safety_signals.meaning: Player momentum and board control; reduces stack height

safety_signals.visual_form: Multiple simultaneous clears in single move

faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Yoshi character portrait; Shy Guy references (unused garbage block graphics with Shy Guy faces)
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Computer opponents from Yoshi's Island cast (Lakitu background in Endless mode)
faction_indicators.environmental_markers: Yoshi's Island-themed backgrounds; pastoral Mario aesthetic

faction_indicators.faction: Fairy faction (Panel de Pon JP original)
faction_indicators.symbol_or_sigil: Fairy character portraits; Lip as protagonist
faction_indicators.enemy_visual_variants: Fantasy fairy characters with distinct color schemes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blocks rise slowly; generous clear space on playfield; enemy garbage blocks minimal or absent
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player has time to plan moves; blocks advance leisurely

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blocks accelerate rise speed; more garbage blocks appear from opponent
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Increased urgency required; combo chains become critical for survival

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Blocks rise rapidly; large volume of gray garbage blocks on-screen; playfield crowding visible
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Player must execute rapid matches; failure imminent if stack reaches boundary

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Opposing player's portrait/character visible; their garbage blocks materialize on player's board
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Damage system active; player combos/clears send garbage to opponent

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Static block arrangement; boundary line marks clearance target; blocks do not rise
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Limited move count; strategic planning emphasized over reflexes

world_state_to_visual.visual_manifestation: Changing background scenery (Lakitu/Windy environment shift) as progression increases
world_state_to_visual.behavioral_manifestation: Continuously accelerating block speed; survival duration measures success

environmental_storytelling.example: Lakitu's background in Tetris Attack Endless Mode; Windy's background in Panel de Pon Endless Mode—establishes opponent identity and world context through visual environment
Mario/Yoshi-universe color scheme vs. fantasy fairy aesthetic—signals franchise/regional identity through visual coherence without narrative exposition
Gray blocks with Shy Guy faces (unused in final build)—reveals intended visual communication of damage source and enemy presence
Block rise speed tied to stage progression—communicates difficulty escalation and time pressure through pure visual motion

entity_behavioral_tells.behavior: Advances upward at stage-dependent speed; becomes hazard if reaching top
Selects and swaps adjacent blocks to form matches; determines match timing and combo chains
Sends garbage blocks to player's board; AI difficulty scales with stage progression
Disappears with animation; triggers cascade falling of blocks above; can chain into additional matches
Occupies space; does not match with colors; must be cleared indirectly by adjacent colored block matches

notes: Tetris Attack is a minimal-narrative puzzle game with world-state communication centered entirely on gameplay mechanics and visual progression rather than story. Danger/safety signals are exclusively mechanical (rising blocks = loss condition, chain reactions = success state). Faction identity differs significantly between Panel de Pon (fantasy fairies, Lip as protagonist) and Tetris Attack (Yoshi's Island cast, Lakitu as stage character). Visual manifestation of difficulty is primary world-state indicator: background changes, block rise acceleration, and garbage block volume signal progression without dialogue or explicit narrative. Unused graphics (Shy Guy garbage faces, thicker garbage blocks) suggest original Panel de Pon design included more explicit enemy/damage visualization that was simplified or removed in localization.
sources_used: tcrf - Tetris Attack (SNES) - The Cutting Room Floor
